name: loger-long-context-3d-reconstruction
description: Scale dense 3D reconstruction to minutes-long video sequences using hybrid memory combining parametric global context with non-parametric local attention. Trains on 128-frame chunks while generalizing to 19,000+ frame sequences, achieving linear complexity for long-horizon geometric understanding tasks.
paperTitle: LoGeR: Long-Context Geometric Reconstruction with Hybrid Memory
content:
## Problem

Dense 3D reconstruction from video degrades catastrophically as sequences grow beyond training length. Global feature alignment drifts across thousands of frames, yet dense local alignment requires uncompressed memory—creating conflicting requirements that standard architectures cannot reconcile.

## Actionable Technique: Dual-Component Hybrid Memory

Combine two complementary memory strategies in unified architecture:

**Parametric Test-Time Training (TTT)**: Maintains compressed global coordinate frames anchoring scale and preventing drift across thousands of frames through lightweight per-sequence optimization.

**Non-parametric Sliding Window Attention (SWA)**: Preserves uncompressed local features for frame-to-frame alignment, keeping inference aligned with training data distribution.

```python
# Hybrid memory forward pass structure
class LoGeRMemory:
    def __init__(self, feature_dim=256, window_size=32):
        self.global_context = LearnableGlobalContext(feature_dim)  # TTT
        self.local_window = SlidingWindowBuffer(window_size, feature_dim)  # SWA

    def update(self, features, frame_idx):
        # Update both simultaneously
        self.local_window.push(features)
        self.global_context.update_with_gradient(features, frame_idx)
        return self.combine(features)

    def combine(self, features):
        """Fuse global anchoring with local precision."""
        local_aligned = self.local_window.align(features)
        global_drift_correction = self.global_context.predict_frame_shift()
        return local_aligned + global_drift_correction
```

This achieves O(n) complexity while maintaining both global consistency and local geometric precision.

## Actionable Technique: Chunk-Wise Processing with Overlapping Boundaries

Decompose long sequences into overlapping chunks processed sequentially:

```python
# Chunk-wise processing strategy
def process_long_sequence(frames, chunk_size=128, overlap=16):
    """Process long sequence as overlapping chunks."""
    chunks = create_overlapping_chunks(frames, chunk_size, overlap)
    results = []

    for chunk_idx, chunk in enumerate(chunks):
        # Process chunk with carry-forward from previous
        result = model(chunk, previous_context=results[-1] if results else None)
        # Keep only non-overlapping portion
        results.append(result[overlap:] if overlap > 0 else result)

    return concatenate(results)
```

## Actionable Technique: Curriculum Training Schedule

Progress through stages increasing sequence length while decreasing chunk density:

**Stage 1 (Warmup)**: Train on 48-frame chunks, single scale, establish basic alignment
**Stage 2 (Extension)**: 96-frame chunks, introduce multi-scale features, moderate data augmentation
**Stage 3 (Generalization)**: 128-frame chunks, full multi-scale hierarchy, curriculum-weighted sampling

This stabilizes recurrent component optimization while enabling generalization beyond training length.

## Actionable Technique: Data Mixture Weighting

Heavily weight large-scale navigation datasets (TartanAirV2 70%, Virtual KITTI 2 20%) to overcome "data wall" preventing long-horizon generalization. Smaller synthetic datasets alone fail to provide sufficient diversity for robust scale drift correction.

## Actionable Technique: Feedforward Alignment Correction (LoGeR*)

Apply SE(3) rigid transformation at chunk boundaries to correct accumulated drift without post-hoc optimization:

```python
# Feedforward rigid alignment
def align_chunk_boundary(features_prev_end, features_curr_start):
    """Compute SE(3) alignment from overlapping frames."""
    # Use overlapping frames to estimate relative pose
    R, t = estimate_rigid_transform(features_prev_end, features_curr_start)
    # Apply to current chunk features
    features_curr_aligned = apply_se3(features_curr_start, R, t)
    return features_curr_aligned
```

## When NOT to use

- Short video clips (<5 seconds) where simpler single-frame methods suffice
- Extremely dynamic non-rigid deformations (water, smoke) where TTT assumptions fail
- Real-time applications demanding <20ms inference per frame

## Hyperparameter Recommendations

| Parameter | Value | Notes |
|-----------|-------|-------|
| TTT learning rate | 0.01 | Per-sequence optimization |
| Window size (SWA) | 32 frames | Balance local precision vs. memory |
| Chunk size (training) | 128 frames | Critical for downstream generalization |
| Curriculum overlap | 16 frames | Enable smooth chunk transitions |
| TartanAir weight | 0.7 | Dominant dataset for scale stability |
| SE(3) refinement iterations | 2-3 | Cost-benefit plateau quickly |

## References

Paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.03269
